So you guys all know the The slogan What happened to this thing? Give a man a fish feed him for a day teach a man to fish Yeah, so the the part of that that often is not quoted is Teach a man to sell fish and you feed him for a lifetime without the cursed monotony of eating only fish so so Copywriting is really and John and I come from exactly the school of Copywriting is salesperson ship that's for the for women's lovers in a room a Salesmanship in print my my book editor corrects So salesperson in ship if she was here She would like the fact that I tried to say that so salesmanship in print is really what copy is Good copywriting is good selling. That's what it is in today's USA today I got it in my room. Do you guys get USA today in your room or am I a privileged person somehow? Okay, it's not such a privilege when you consider the front page headlines, but The so in USA today today there is a full-page ad for Contests they're running Where you get to do an ad for USA today, thank you. There you go. Yeah. Yeah, let me have it so Very good say now anybody ever tells you you aren't useful you I Can testify so here's this ad Hey, by the way, this in case you didn't know it. This is USA today's new logo which about a month ago This was front-page news that they had a new logo a blue dot Which by the way, I will bet you two years of meetings about the circumference of the dot Should it be blue should I promise you I've sat in these meetings. So here's this ad Give us your creative win a million dollars in advertising. You may want to enter this until I tell you why you shouldn't So you get to do an ad for USA today Okay, it's the 2012 print ad competition. Okay, so here's your first problem First problem is who the judges are so it's judged by some of the best names in the business what business? so number one is Andrew Essex the CEO of drag a five number two is chip kid the designer and writer at Noff double-day publishing group Which is a book publisher? Okay, not only don't they know anything about advertising. They don't even know how to sell books Their printers is what they are Three Sean McLaughlin the creative director of an agency time on take the co-founder and co-CEO of co-collective He's a co-co-co-co Co thing so they can't even make a decision about who's supposed to run the place and he's going to make a decision about your advertising Chuck Porter the chairman of another agency and Michael Wolf the media columnist of USA today All right, so your first problem is you got judges that don't know anything about how to sell anything Who are not going to pass judgment on whatever it is you do, but it gets worse Here is their statement Underneath the 2012 print ad competition here it is you'll this is a writer down or you want to write this down Print advertising is an art and you're the artist They couldn't be more wrong Art is art We are not sure what that is Some people claim to know it when they see it at the National Endowment of the Arts we give money for pictures of people peeing on crosses and we call that art so we have a wide range of what we consider to be art But anybody who thinks advertising is art is a moron The point of advertising is not to have somebody like it so much They wish to put it in a frame and put it up on their wall That's not the point of advertising the point of advertising is to sell something and that bunch Undoubtedly does not think so so they will judge it on criteria that has nothing to do with whether or not it is capable of selling anything most of you in this room live in the real world Which is where John and I and our clients live which often is Advertising bought for a dollar with a hot check and must produce revenue before check clears That's that's the real world. It's definitely the world. I grew up in that's how I came up Buy postage stamps with hot check drop stuff in mail Hope money arrives before check clears. It's a little trick you used to be able to do you can't do it now You still take an exacto knife and you used to be able to cut through the but the numbers on the bottom of the check That bought you an extra four or five days some of the people in the room are not and other people don't know that but that Bought you another four or five days. I bet I didn't issue a check for three years that I didn't put it in a razor blade Put a little things right, okay, so I mean you talk about hope as a strategy. We were running on that So so so so when that's the world you live in It ain't art Unless it's about a guy named art it ain't art. It's about selling stuff and so when you get it that copy is selling and Selling is really copy Then you're a long way towards being able to either produce for yourself or know when you see it produced for you effective copy There's very simple test If you stood up on stage and read it aloud Would people run to the back of the room and buy things simple test when you read it aloud to yourself? Is this what you would say to somebody if you were face-to-face with them across the kitchen table or across the conference table trying to sell them something? And if it's not it's probably not good Copy that's pretty simple, but we can make this much more complicated and since we have three sessions to fill tonight Tomorrow morning and the next night I am about to make it much more complicated for you There is actually there is a secret code and So my contribution tonight will be to talk about the secret code John's contribution tonight incidentally will be and people ask about this Incessively will be about unique sales proposition What a good one is and how to create it? tomorrow morning by the way John and I are going to be on stage together and try and annoy each other and And try and find our way to some meaningful and significant stuff for you in conversation And then the next session I forget what I'm doing, but I do remember what John's doing And John is doing how to create a damn good ad in nine minutes Which will be very useful to those of you who like want to work the four hour week because you don't have a lot of time to work with so Not on nine minute ads kind of essential. So it's sort of the companion to Tim Ferriss's book. So There is a secret code to successful Info marketing copy in just about every Category there is so for categories by the way, let me just jump to that for a second Info marketing. I mean, there's a million little ones But basically the categories that the info marketing business really deals with is health And in there we find fitness we find beauty we find weight loss There's finance in where we find the investment stuff the personal finance stuff The how to make money in stocks how to make money in commodities how to make money in real estate how to make money in llamas and The doom and gloom are begetting the end of the world the kind of the Glenn Beck crowd. There's the There's the opportunity category Okay, there's the niche business category which our panel was all about this morning There's personal growth and self-approval and there's hobbies, okay, and that pretty much Are is is the list of the main categories in info marketing? So there is a secret copy code behind Every one of those categories Whichever one or one supply to you? I'm going to tell you how to go crack the code for yourself and how to reverse the code into a template From which you can rather efficiently Create for the most part serviceable copy now. It may not be John Carlton level copy It may not be Dan Kennedy level copy, but it will be serviceable copy for most of your purposes in most of your places You will be able to get good and effective results working from this process Crack the code convert the code to a template and then write to fit the template. So that's what we're going to do Here's why it's necessary The reason we want to steal other people's copy codes is Because well, oh does that disturb some of you you're giggling at the word steal There's ethical theft and there's unethical theft Where's Nelson Cersei the he's in my titanium group. He's a pastor. Okay? Just just tune it out. Okay Well the good thing of having the good part about having them and I in a titanium group It's pretty much everybody else in the titanium group needs forgiveness on a regular frequent and consistent basis So, you know, we have him there and he sprinkles some water on us and we leave and it's okay. So So there's but seriously there's ethical theft and unethical theft so in this case so unethical theft is like verb theft of copy verbatim Okay for that You should go to jail and you actually can go to jail if somebody's very aggressive about it I'm talking about stealing the architecture I'm talking about stealing the framework on which you now hang the words. I consider that ethical theft So the reason why it's necessary to steal other people's copy codes is Because in most cases most of you can't do enough testing to genuinely get to success by your own testing So the big guys In any category do mammoth Amounts of test they do big horizontal tests 20 30 different headlines This is like the beginning the beginning of a test matrix This is why you have to steal code So if you got if we're going to test for less And we're going to test 5,000 names on each of the four lists of we're going to do direct mail We'll just start with this. So we have headline a headline B Now we have offer a which we have to test with headline a but we also have to test offer a with headline B Then we have price a which we have to test with headline a with offer a but then we have Price a we have to test with headline a with offer B. Etc. Etc. Etc. Etc. Etc So just this far which is not a complete test at 5,000 at 5,000 units of cell I'm up to 400,000 pieces in the mail in order to run the test most of you are niches that don't even have 400,000 customers in the first place So if we try and build our own code from scratch through our own testing we either won't live that long Okay, some of us are closer to the end than others, but I'm telling you nobody in here will live that long We'll get weary will go broke Right one of those bad things is going to happen long before we crack the code through our own devices So we want to steal code from somebody who is able to do a lot of testing with the people We want to sell to preferably with something comparable to what it is that we want to sell And their code the good news is For you that once you identify who they are they can't hide their code They can't They either put it in a mail they put it in print advertising They put it up on the web. They send it out in email, which you can sign up to get they can't hide it now. It's Buried but it can be unearthed from what it is that they do so the example I'm going to show you I deliberately picked the category That I that very few people in the room if any are in so that none of you have a bias About your own experience in this category But understand whatever category or categories are most Relevant are closest to you. You want to go due to them what I'm going to show you I did to this one So this is and you have this now on your little crack the code sheet where we're going to go next So this is the code behind the investment newsletter part of our industry Which you should know it's they're privately held companies for the most part So it's hard to get a grip on it But it is at least a billion dollar a year industry It may be closer to a ten billion dollar a year industry Nobody's really sure but it's big One of our exhibitors here everbank lives in that world with those publishers by the way and does very well with the Mary Ellen Tribee who some of you have heard speak here came from that world so that world is the it's the Armageddon guys Who publish newsletters about the end of the economy as we know it and what you're supposed to do to cope with it Which by the way, they all had a very good night the other night Because they have four more. I'm serious They have four more really good years ahead of them of mailing the same sales letters They've been mailing for the last four years Some of them see on the summit only come out When these circumstances reoccur Howard rough if anybody knows Howard Howard's in that world and the last time I looked Howard If he's still alive, I don't check every day, but the last time well, I got caught on one today We were in a meeting and I mentioned somebody and I said, you know, he's very bad health problems and somebody said he's dead I said well see um Carl said I asked me if I felt bad about it. I said no, I just feel really good about being right um So Howard if he's alive is somewhere between 150 and 190 years old And Howard's been retired. I don't know how many times But when kind of an economy like we just had happens when a situation would people feel threatened by the government Howard can't resist he reappears and gets back in the newsletter business and he takes sales letters He hasn't used in 20 years and he changes the dates and he puts it back in the mail and they work great So this is a billion-dollar industry sometimes. It's a ten billion-dollar year industry other times, but it's big if you're unfamiliar with it All you got to do is find one subscribe to one You will soon be getting all of the mail from all of them because everybody of course rents and shares everybody else's Lists in that category. So they are all information marketers Almost all their businesses revolve around a newsletter Most of you are familiar with the end of America campaign that went on this this past year So the businesses revolve around newsletters some of them revolve around books and courses many of them then have Ascension models that are not very different from what you're used to seeing with us They put people into seminars and conferences. They put people into different levels of membership They often have five or six brands under one roof that they are running a Gora Phillips those guys All right, so the investment newsletter Category so here's their code now I want you to understand how I got their code and I'm going to show you some examples I Did what you will do right is I took all their stuff And I Napoleon Hildett right I hunted for the commonalities And it's and I stick counted the commonalities out of six bazillion pieces of their direct mail And I created a code not in any particular order by the way. We'll get to that so their code Is as follows it includes again, these are not in priority order They're just numbered to make it easier to reference them one is alarm. Okay, so they all use the psychological trigger of alarm There are some impending disaster just around the corner the black helicopters are coming all of your stuff is going to be confiscated There'll be no food. There'll be no water. There'll be no colloidal silver. They'll be no whatever and you got to get some right now Okay Many of them recently Lee Bellinger who's very big in this business. They're selling one of their back-end products I think it's five hundred ninety nine dollars is a pre-packaged get out of your house in 90 minutes kit Okay, and so it's got an axe and a box of band-aids and some dried food and a flashlight And all put together in a box. Okay, that's one of their back-end products. Okay, so they they live on Alarm you will not find hardly any Pitch no matter how positive it is otherwise where they have not used alarm somewhere in it in this entire industry Second mystery and mystique this industry is very big on the secrets They don't want you to know The only difference is really who they are But there is a they Who does not want you to know things? That's important three amazing stories of people like the reader So whoever it's aimed at there's going to be stories of people just like them people They can identify with people who appear to be by description ordinary normal everyday folk Who are using the super XYZ formula to make money even as the economy crashes around them for? authority figure expert and media verification Somebody verifies what it is that they're saying five open acknowledgment that what they are saying is not to be believed That it's strange that it's weird that it's G if this is true How come you're the first person I'm hearing it from that doesn't make sense to me? They openly acknowledge that in almost every piece of copy you will find in this industry This piece exists again. We're looking some examples in a second six They answer the question of why this is different because it must be different Donna Kretz spoke this morning really about the point of differentiation This must be different than everything that has gone before because This is not the first piece of mail. This is not the first webinar This is not the first email you're getting in this category if you're a buyer in this category You're getting a lot of it like every day So it must be different Okay, your enemy is my enemy. Thus, I'm on your side You will find that in almost every case if it's good enough for somebody famous Then it's good enough for you pronouncements of epic and profound importance You won't find these people being subtle or talking about small things. They will talk about big This changes everything Sort of things 10 some sort of emotional mission statement a Reason why somebody is there is showing up now to tell you this story to offer you this proposition Pretty much without a mission statement story or an origin story. It doesn't make sense Hey, Batman is a fine example. How many know the Batman origin story? Okay Now keep them up if you're a diehard comic book fan and like you paid attention to Batman your whole life Not that many so how come they know the origin story? Because it's been told over and over and over again to make sure everybody gets it because otherwise he don't make any sense Otherwise, he's a freak in a costume dressed up like a bat running around beating the crap out everybody This is not a heroic thing. This is a scary thing This is not good without the origin story. So there's an origin story crisis to opportunity and or privilege so yes, there's a crisis That's the alarm But if you know certain things if you're in the right place if you're connected to the right person It really means opportunity 12 some sort of exotic unusual proprietary named solution All right, we have to rename everything in this field. Which my answer is by the way, let's order chicken see I already know what I don't need but so that's important 14 the what to do without the how to do it and And timeliness Recency and urgency so now quickly just so you see this keep your little list in front of you because I only have numbers So here is a control right now For in in this category, okay? We're telling you to buy But it does make them different Eight on your list if it's good enough for George Bush's secret retirement legacy Okay, turns out George Bush has got one of these plans. How about that? Okay, next letter. This is a different one same code different letter on December 12 2012 or December 21 2012 America's most popular investment will reach its use by date It will be a civilization altering development That's number nine on your code list number four a reviewer in newsweek And the wall street journal now what's funny about this verification. This is what's called negative verification This is because they attack me. I must be real So a reviewer in newsweek dismissed this has an unthinking attack on reason And the wall street journal categorically denounced it has no more than the natterings of a dopey ant So if you're worried about your negative reviews This is what to do with them You use them as verification Because if you're being attacked you must know something pretty cool 11 By the way, that's a lot easier to do than continuing to shove stuff to push it further down in the google listings Just take it off of there and use it when somebody calls you a nattering ant number 11 Crisis equals opportunity, but if you were on the right side You could seize opportunities that only come along once or twice in a century. The mother of all financial bubbles is just now starting to pop Then we have our number 14 14 is what to do not how to do it Okay, so here's what you need to do step one make sure you live and work someplace safe And then it tells us about safe places doesn't tell us where they are Doesn't tell us how to get there, but it tells us we should be living in a safe place Step number two move your money out of harm's way Okay, it makes sense. He didn't know no, he had it. Okay, that's That is what to not how to Okay, here's another control. Here's number eight This is caught on videotape footage from recent business. Here's number eight Including the vice president of chevron and even the managers of 125 million dollar pension plan in san francisco All right, so we've got if it's good enough for them It's good enough for Okay, so there's your code list from this industry now If you take any category of info marketing So if you take niche the guys who are up the folks are up here this morning if you take niches So if we took all their marketing And we lined it up on a table and we carefully went through and we identified something that they all did And we wrote it down. We would crack their code. So here's how to crack any code Number one, you need at least 12 to 24 historical controls You don't just want contemporary because sometimes the current people drift away From what works so you want a dozen to 24 historical controls All right, so I go looking if I enter a new category whether it's an info marketing or in something else I go looking for the history Of what was being sold in that category five years ago 10 years ago 20 years ago And I try and find some controls that have history I will at least go say to the trade journals So like i'm rewriting right now lead generation ads in the chiropractic field for For dr. Tom Tomshack at health source who's a franchise or I wrote his first lead generation ads He's been living on them for a few years. They're starting to wane. I now got to write them again So even though I know a lot I wanted every chiropractic trade journal for the past two years So I could go through and I could find my controls and I could make sure I had the code list right Before I wrote the copy. So we want historical controls. Then we want 6 to 12 contemporary controls. So we want to find somebody We want to find the people who are selling Preferably the bigger players because we know they're doing a lot of testing To the buyers we're going to be selling to and who are selling something for For which they are going to need to make the same kind of pitch We would need to make understand it doesn't have to be exactly So in the opportunity category if I sell How to get rich in the carpet cleaning business. I don't just need carpet cleaning Ron LeGrand stuff about how to get rich in real estate counts Okay, his controls count because the premise is how to get rich What the shovel we do it with is really not particularly relevant when you write copy You can kind of plug that in at the last minute. So I want historical controls And I want contemporary controls That we sit down with them and we search for the commonalities Oh look, every one of these has tastes like chicken. Okay, I'm gonna write down tastes like chicken All right, so we're looking for the commonalities. That's where the code list Comes from now if this sounds arduous by the way, I built this one For the financial newsletters. I did it for I didn't do it just for you. I got paid I did it when we started to do a lot of work two years ago for the financial advisors And I updated it again about three months ago for the financial advisors because they Although they don't sell newsletters And they don't sell books. They actually want clients for whom they're going to manage money or from whom they're going to take money okay, manage Some yes some no But they're at least going to take it However, they're selling to the same client the same person that these guys are selling to and they're basically selling the same Premise is you need to do something different with your money than you're doing now and you need to do it quick So these guys are doing all kind of testing my local advisor in scott's dale, arizona can't do that kind of testing So i've got to steal this code and use it for Him you get it So we're going to identify commonalities fourth then we create our code list just like I created a list That's going to govern All of our copywriting then if you want to get real Sophistic if you want to do a little work so you it's faster to do a lot of work Then you create a swipe file for each Thing on your code list so I can now go back to a drawer and I can find Pull a file that just says alarm at the top and has all the alarm stuff copied and put in there And I can go pull the taste like chicken file folder You can do this on your computer if you like I just like paper But and in there is all the taste like chicken stuff I've cut it up. I've copied it and I put it in my file folders So I have a swipe file for every item that is on my code list All right now the criteria Of a code owner worth watching because you don't want to be random about this number one They got to be doing direct response. They can't be doing brand or image stuff They have to be doing real honest to god direct response Secondly, it's better if you if they are they could be doing online and they can be doing broadcast But it's better if they are doing print and direct mail to Because they're spending more money doing print and direct mail than they are online Therefore they tend to invest more in it In case you haven't noticed Most of the email you get is not as well crafted as most of the mail you get Now that's not because it's necessarily less important. It's just because people have an attitude about it It's cheap to send out And we usually do it quick in at the last minute So nobody wants to pay a copywriter $50,000 to write an email They're happy to pay a copywriter $50,000 to write a print ad So you want to study the print ads more than you study the email Ideally you want it to be a company of size so that they can be doing a lot of testing They can be doing the testing you can Number four Obviously you want them active in your category or a comparable category and number five you want some longevity Some evidence they've been running multiple promotions over a period of time because you really don't want To be following an amateur around who is learning Day to day just as you are so how to find the code owners Look in the specialty interest publications that your readers that your customers that your buyers read Find code owners ads answer them Buy from them if you wish If you must get all their stuff In a niche Or a subculture if you are not in the niche you are not in the subculture So you are let's see who is up here who's not Well really they're all from their niches, but let's take uh Let's take rory fat at restaurant marketing systems who never owned a restaurant So what rory would do is he needs a buddy who owns a restaurant Because he needs three months of that guy's junk mail If you don't have a buddy just go pay somebody They put a big laundry basket in their office or their house and they throw all their junk mail in it And at the end of three months you give them a buck for every piece of junk mail They threw in the big laundry basket and you got three months worth of junk mail and they're excited And now you can take it all home and you can sift it into piles And you can check carefully to see who's these five pieces are really from the same place Okay, and this one came three times during the month that probably means it's a control And you can now create your tabletop from which you are going to crack the code Okay, there's a book called the directory of major mailers Which you can get which tells you who's mailing how much to whom Then you can go google them And you can find your website and you can find your address and you can buy something from them And then you'll be on their mailing list plus you'll be on the mailing list of everybody They rent their mailing lists to and bingo you will soon have a big table Full of stuff that you can begin to sift and sort and use to crack the code There's a reference called standard rating data service s rds.com. It's a commercial marketplace of mailing lists Again, it will tell you Who's got what size list how many new names they get every 90 days? How many come from direct mail? How many come from these other sources when you find somebody who is very Anagolous to you that you want to model there's another thing you can do You can act like you want to rent that list whether you do or not And you are then going to get a thing called a data card and one of the things that's going to tell you Is who's renting that list on a continuous basis? You then can go to the inside direct mail archives and you can order the controls of everybody on that list And they make xeroxes. I'm and send them to you. You know, you have to go find them And now if you so if we go find the investors business daily master file Of people who not only subscribe to investors business daily, but buy stuff from investors their best list We see there's eight companies that are mailing it all the time We then go to inside direct mail and we say give me the current controls of these eight companies bingo We got them and now we can search them for commonalities and we can crack the code Not only can we crack the code in general in that category? We've cracked the code of who's selling to that list And if we're going to mail to that list or advertise in that publication We can if we'll do a little work have a code list just for it Nanton dicks if you're in the room, this is part of the conversation We had earlier today Why the same ad really shouldn't run in four different publications without some changes because there may be different code Websites of course we can go to their websites We can sign up for their email lists. We can go to their webinars, etc And of course buy from the best spend a little money now. How do we turn all this into a template? so There's two approaches to templating So first of all you can decide sort of arbitrarily on the big architecture you wish to use for a copy I happen to like I like to use the template to validate the problem Agitate problem invalidate options solve more than I like anything else Okay, so if you examine my work and you want to look for an architecture code, you'll find that pretty quick You'll see I use it over and over and I just hang different clothes on it. Okay, but the architecture is the same Okay, some people like the oldest sales form of tension So you can go down an architecture then you can take your code items from your code list and put them in order in the architecture What do you think should go first? Under attention. What do you think should go first under interest? What do you think should go first under desire and you can line them up? Then you can create your headlines and your subheads from that and bingo. Guess what you got You got an outline. You can also take anybody's control So if you find if you go through the whole exercise I just did in the investment newsletter category and we identify now That list which I gave you as a sample the code list If we can find a control that has all those items in it We can convert it to a template. So if you'll flip your little hand out over You will see Okay So this is the first page of that control that I showed you for the 801 k plans Right and at the bottom I have converted it into a template To write a sales letter from it. The template is there's a pre headline There's a main headline which uses item 12 and 13 on my code list The pre headline uses number one on my code list. There's a secondary headline which uses number nine on my code list There's an intro paragraph above the salutation Which uses number two plus four on my code list opening paragraphs use five nine and 12 And the paragraphs in the latter part of the first page use number three I now have a template If I'm going to write to these same buyers I can write a sales letter to fit that template All I need is my template And my code list Oh, i'm going to write a pre headline and i'm going to use number one. What's number one alarm. Okay. I need an alarm Pre headline for what i'm selling All right, you get now if we could go through the whole letter page by page by page Which I did not do for you And convert every page into a template just like I did here And we could then go back and write a sales letter For our investment newsletter Built on that same Successful template that has already come from an enormous amount of testing that we either aren't able to do Or don't want to do or don't want to live long enough to do Now if this sounds arduous to you If you're going to generate a lot of copy and if you're going to generate a lot of promotions It's less arduous a lot less arduous than starting from scratch Every time you're going to write copy Yeah, this takes a little time to build But once it's built You get to use it over and over and over Again because it doesn't wear out So as The reason freelance copywriters those of us who make our living Pretty much writing copy like john and I We tend at least over time if not initially to get pretty narrow in the categories in which we will write And we turn down a lot of other things The reason for that is sloth on his part and efficiency on mine so The reason for that is because we got templates In that category where we have already done a lot of work We've got copy banks, which i'm going to talk about in a second In that category So if you want me to do skincare See i've got templates for that I've already got copy banks for that. I've already done all this For that and I just get to use it again and again And again So the pro copywriters especially the high income ones they tend to wind up only writing copy in three or four different Categories of products or services because they want to reuse this stuff over and over and over again The real meticulous ones may check it like I just did in chris's case But the check was pretty quick because I already had templates And swipe files tied to my code. I got my code and I got a copy bank So the last piece of this puzzle is a copy bank. So the copy bank now is You've decided on your categories. You've built templates write copy hunks From your code for every one of those categories lots of them Just anytime you've got a few spare minutes Write some copy. You don't have to need it this minute So get 500 opening paragraphs that use alarm Done that have some relationship to your business and have them in a box or a computer file Now you got a copy bank. You definitely want a vocabulary list a good copy bank has a pre done vocabulary list What it's like your own the czaras. What are the words you should use to describe? Armageddon, what are the words you should use to describe opportunity? What are the words? So Now again when a pro builds that So I've built that for clients the last one I built build at a million dollars So this is worth You taking the time to do if you're going to do a lot of copy in your category Or if you're going to have copywriters work for you Because if you can hand them a copy bank to work from you make everybody's life a lot easier and a lot more efficient You organize your swipe material by each one of these categories And ideally you got one item on each card on a card on a piece of paper In a computer file so you can print out just the one thing that you want You want your copy bank ultimately to work like a chinese menu So it is now time to write I pick or build my template And then I pick one item from column a and one item from column b And one item from column c and I stick them in the template and then I smooth out the edges That at least gets you to rough draft if not to finished draft So i'm not sure in john's case I've asked a bunch of pro copywriters freelancers how many drafts they do and many of them say to be four or five six seven eight drafts I never do more than two never I do a first draft and a final draft. That's what I do The first draft is what I just described to you in most of the cases It's chinese menued into a template smooth out the edges The final draft is finishing it off making this sound better You know, oh, I got an inspiration for an idea. I can fit in here first draft final draft out the door That's the power of a great copy bank. So how many think this is cool? Okay, good very good. Okay It's only cool if you go do it Okay, all right, so before we take a quick stretch break. I have to squeeze in a commercial announcement Um, so something's happening next year that you get to be the first people to know about it And therefore get to save some money if you wish to do it.
